title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
summary: A courageous girl journeys to the ends of the earth to rescue her love from a cruel enchantment.
full_text:
In a small, wind-swept cottage in the far North, where the winters were long and the frost bit deep into the earth, there lived a widow with three daughters. The eldest two were proud and lazy, spending their days dreaming of wealth and mocking their mother's worn hands. Only the youngest was kind and good, spending her hours tending to the hearth and comfort of her family. One winter night, when the aurora borealis danced in ribbons of green and violet across the sky, a great white bear came to their door. His eyes were like polished amber, and his voice was a low rumble that shook the floorboards. 'Give me your youngest daughter,' the bear demanded, 'and I will make you rich beyond your wildest dreams. Your house will be filled with gold, and your daughters will wear silks from the East.' The elder sisters, consumed by greed, pushed the youngest forward, practically throwing her into the bear's massive paws. Away she went, disappearing into the white blur of the blizzard.

The girl discovered that the bear was not a beast of prey, but a guardian. He led her to a grand castle carved from ice and obsidian, filled with silver furniture and gold-leafed walls. In this strange place, she lived in luxury, yet there was a shadow over her happiness. Every night, the bear would come to her—but only in complete darkness. He was tender and loving, treating her as his wife, but as soon as the first light of dawn touched the horizon, he would vanish, leaving her alone in the silent halls. For years, she lived in this golden cage, her heart aching for the sight of the man who loved her in the dark.

Driven by a longing she could no longer contain, the girl decided she must see him by day. She hid a candle beneath her pillow and, at midnight, lit it, casting a flickering glow across the room. When she looked, she saw not a bear, but the most beautiful prince she had ever seen, his face etched with a profound sadness. 'You have broken the spell!' he cried, his voice breaking. 'Because you have looked upon me, the enchantment is not fully broken—it is merely shifted. Now I must go to the castle of the North Wind, east of the sun and west of the moon, to marry a troll-princess. If you do not find me before the stars fall from the sky, I will be lost to the darkness forever.' With those words, he vanished into the morning mist.

Determined to save him, the girl began a journey that would take her beyond the ends of the earth. She searched for three years, walking through frozen wastes and crossing oceans of ice. She met the East Wind, who told her she was too small, and the West Wind, who laughed at her persistence. But the North Wind, seeing the purity of her love, took pity on her. He carried her on his freezing breath to the castle of the trolls. There, she found a fortress with twelve windows. She looked into each one, and in the last, through walls of thick ice and drifting snow, she saw her prince, pale and fading. She pressed her face to the glass, her warm breath melting the frost, and whispered his name. The glass shattered with the force of her love. They embraced, wept, and married that very hour, the curse finally broken by the courage of a heart that refused to give up.

The prince's brother, who had also been cursed, was released through her kindness. The two queens lived in the northern castle, bringing warmth and light to the icy lands, and they remained happy ever after, their love a beacon for all who dared to journey east of the sun and west of the moon.
moral: True love is not found in luxury or convenience, but in the courage to seek it across the furthest horizons.
source: Peter Christen Asbjørnsen and Jørgen Moe

title: Excalibur
summary: In the time of kings, there was a boy named Arthur who pulled a sword from a stone—proving he was the rightful ruler of Britain.
full_text:
In the time of kings, there was a boy named Arthur who pulled a sword from a stone—proving he was the rightful ruler of Britain. He became king, but his sword was plain, forged by mortal hands.

In his first great battle, Arthur faced King Pellinore. Their swords clashed—and Arthur's broke beneath him. Pellinore could have struck the killing blow, but Merlin intervened with sleep, saving them both.

You need a true sword,' Merlin said. Come with me.' He led Arthur to a mystical lake, its waters silver, its shores pearl-bright. Upon its waters, a lady rose—dressed in clouds, holding a sword that blazed like fire.

I am the Lady of the Lake,' she spoke. This is Excalibur—the Sword of the Lake. I give it freely, but hear my condition: you must grant me any boon I ask, should I call upon you.'

Arthur agreed. He waded into the waters and took Excalibur from the ladys hand. The sword blazed so bright it lit the darkness like thirty torches. The arm vanished beneath the lake.

But Excalibur's greatest magic was not the blade—it was the scabbard. Wear it, and no wound would bleed. The scabbard is worth ten of the sword,' Merlin warned. Keep it close.'

Arthurs sister, Morgan le Fay, learned magic in Avalon. She coveted power and stole the scabbard, giving Excalibur's blade to her lover, Accolon. In their duel, Arthurs sword broke—he carried only a counterfeit. He knew truth by weight alone.

Morgan cast the scabbard into a lake, drowning its protection. Arthur was doomed.

When Arthur fell wounded from battle with Mordred, his final command was clear: 'Return Excalibur to the Lake. Sir Bedivere carried the sword to the water's edge—but it was so beautiful, so powerful, he could not throw it away.

Twice he hesitated. Twice he hid the sword. The dying king saw the clouds darken, knowing betrayal.

On the third command, Bedivere hurled Excalibur. A white hand rose from the lake, caught the sword, brandished it three times toward heaven—then vanished beneath the waters forever.

Some powers must be earned. Some must be returned. That is the way of magic.
moral: Great power must be used wisely, and some debts must be paid.
source: Based on Thomas Malorys Le Morte d'Arthur (1485), Geoffrey of Monmouths History of the Kings of Britain (1136), and the Vulgate Cycle

title: El Chupacabra
summary: The goat-sucking creature of Puerto Rican legend, blamed for mysterious livestock deaths.
full_text:
In the hills and pastures of rural Puerto Rico, there is a creature that has terrified farmers since the 1990s. It stalks the night, killing livestock in ways that no ordinary predator can explain. The locals call it El Chupacabra—the Goat Sucker.

Its victims are found drained of blood, their bodies marked with two small puncture wounds on the neck. The goats or chickens that die have no blood left in their bodies—but nothing ate the flesh. Even more strangely, the animals show no signs of struggle. It is as if they were willingly drained by some invisible force.

Descriptions vary. Some say El Chupacabra looks like a large dog or wolf—but with spines instead of fur, like a reptile. Others say it's a hairless kangaroo with sharp claws. Others describe it as a bipedal creature with large red eyes and a snout filled with sharp teeth. The most common report describes a creature the size of a large dog, but with the ability to leap enormous distances in a single bound.

It appears only inPuerto Rico—or does it? Reports have come fromMexico, fromthe American Southwest, from Texas. Some blame secret government experiments. Others say its an alien, escaped fromone of their crash-landed ships. Still others say it's a demon—sent to punish the faithless.

Farmers have tried everything to protect their livestock: silver bullets, holywater, iron. Nothing works. El Chupacabra returns night after night, taking what it wants.

The lesson: In a world with unexplained mysteries, the wisest are those who keep their doubts—and their goats—close.

(But what is El Chupacabra really? Some say it is an evolved mongoose species, escaped froma research facility. Others say its a government experiment gone wrong. No one knows for certain.)
moral: Fear the unknown—but also investigate mystery with reason.

title: Each Uisge
summary: In the Scottish Highlands, the water spirits called Each Uisge haunt the fords and bridges of the wild rivers.
full_text:
In the Scottish Highlands, the water spirits called Each Uisge haunt the fords and bridges of the wild rivers. They appear as beautiful horses, their manes dripping with waterweed, their eyes luminous and cold. If you are the last person to cross a river at night, they will take you.

In a small village in the glens, a woman named Morag knew this rule well. Every evening, she was always the last to cross the old stone bridge over the River Spean, coming home from the fields where she worked. She knew the Each Uisge was waiting.

One evening, as the autumn sun set early behind the mountains, Morag prepared. She took her father's walking stick—a powerful oak branch, thick and strong, carved with ancient symbols by her grandfather. She kissed her children goodbye and set out.

The bridge loomed in the twilight, its stones slick with mist. The river roared below, swollen with rain. As she stepped onto the bridge, she heard it—a splash behind her, soft and deliberate. She did not turn.

She walked faster. The splashing grew louder. Something was emerging from the water.

She called out, her voice steady: "Each Uisge, I know you are there. I am the last to cross, as always. Will you take me?"

The water spirit did not answer. But she felt its presence behind her—cold, patient, hungry.

She gripped the oak stick and kept walking. Halfway across the bridge, she heard a whinny behind her—not a horse's whinny, but something older, wetter, wrong. She spun around.

The Each Uisge stood behind her, its shape shifting between horse and something else, its skin glistening with river slime, its eyes burning with an ancient hunger. It opened its mouth—a mouth too wide, filled with teeth that should not belong to a horse.

Morag raised the oak stick. "In the name of my father and his father before him, I cross this bridge. You have no power over courage."

She slammed the stick against the stone. Sparks flew. The Each Uisge recoiled. In that moment, she ran—her heart pounding, her feet finding the end of the bridge, the solid ground of home.

She reached the other side and turned. The water spirit stood at the bridge's edge, watching her. It did not follow. It could not cross running water, some say. Others say it was defeated by courage.

From that night on, Morag was never last to cross again. She made sure someone always walked with her. But the lesson remained: fear faced is fear conquered. The Each Uisge still waits at fords and bridges, but only for those who let fear freeze their feet.

In Scottish folklore, the Each Uisge was considered the most dangerous of the water horses, more treacherous even than the kelpie of the lochs. While the kelpie lured its victims in still waters, the Each Uisge haunted fast-moving rivers and fords, appearing as a gentle horse to the unwary traveler. Once mounted, its skin became adhesive, and the rider could not dismount. The creature would then gallop into the deepest part of the river, drowning its victim and devouring them. Only the eyes and liver were left behind. To this day, Highlanders warn strangers to be wary of lone horses near river crossings, for not every beautiful creature is what it seems.
moral: The last is brave; fear can be faced.

title: El Dorado
summary: High in the cold, windswept plateau of the Colombian Andes, there lies a lake of dark, still water, ringed by ancient mountains and shrouded in mist.
full_text:
High in the cold, windswept plateau of the Colombian Andes, there lies a lake of dark, still water, ringed by ancient mountains and shrouded in mist. Lake Guatavita, they call it now, but to the Muisca people who lived along its shores for centuries before the arrival of the Spanish, it was a sacred place, a portal to the underworld where the gods could be reached and honored. And it was here, in a ceremony of breathtaking beauty and profound spiritual meaning, that one of the most seductive legends in human history was born: the legend of El Dorado, the city of gold.

The Muisca people had no cities of gold, no streets paved with precious metals. They were a sophisticated civilization of farmers, weavers, and goldsmiths who lived in the highlands of what is now Colombia. Gold, to them, was not currency but something far more sacred—a material that captured the light of the sun, the gift of the gods, to be offered back to them in times of great importance. And the greatest of these occasions was the crowning of a new chief, or zipa.

When a new zipa was to be consecrated, the ceremony began days before. The chief-to-be would be taken to the shores of Lake Guatavita, where priests cleansed him with sacred herbs and prayers. Then came the moment that would, centuries later, drive men mad with greed. The chief was stripped naked and his entire body was covered in a sticky resin made from tree sap. Then, with great reverence, the priests blew gold dust over him through hollow tubes of reeds, until he shimmered from head to foot like a god made of sunlight. They called him "El Dorado"—the Gilded One.

Thus transformed into a living statue of gold, the zipa was taken onto a raft woven from rushes, a craft large enough to hold him and the priests who accompanied him. The raft was piled high with offerings: golden figurines of animals and men, emeralds the size of pigeon eggs, precious stones, and intricately crafted jewelry. As the raft drifted into the center of the lake, the priests began to chant, their voices echoing off the surrounding mountains. Then the zipa, the Gilded One, dove into the cold, dark water. The gold dust washed off his body and sank into the depths, an offering to the god who lived beneath the lake. One by one, the priests cast the golden offerings into the water after him. The ceremony was complete. The chief was now consecrated, carrying the blessings of the gods back to his people.

When the Spanish conquistadors first heard whispers of this ceremony in the 1530s, their imaginations were fired by something far more powerful than reverence. Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada, who led the first major expedition into Muisca territory, heard the stories and understood them through a lens of European greed. The gilded man became a gilded city. The sacred raft became a treasure ship. Lake Guatavita became the location of a lost city of gold, its streets paved with gold, its buildings encrusted with jewels.

The legend grew with each retelling. Expeditions were launched, each more desperate and destructive than the last. In 1545, conquistadors attempted to drain Lake Guatavita by cutting a notch in the rim of the crater, lowering the water level enough to reach the treasures they believed lay at the bottom. They recovered hundreds of golden artifacts, but the water was still too deep, and the slippery crater walls made further excavation impossible. A more ambitious attempt was made in the late 16th century, and again in the 19th century by a British company that tried to tunnel into the lake bed. They found more gold, but never the fabled city.

The search for El Dorado consumed lives. It drove men to madness, starvation, and death in the jungles of South America. Sir Walter Raleigh, the great English explorer, made two expeditions up the Orinoco River in search of what he called "the great and golden city of Manoa," which he believed to be El Dorado. He returned empty-handed both times and was eventually executed for his failures. The legend that had promised infinite wealth had delivered only tragedy.

And yet, the Muisca gold was real. The famous Muisca Raft, a golden model of the ceremony discovered in 1969, now resides in the Gold Museum in Bogotá, a stunning testament to the artistry of a people who saw gold not as wealth but as a sacred offering. The true treasure of El Dorado was never the gold that the Spanish sought. It was the civilization they destroyed in their greed, the culture they could not comprehend, the ceremony that meant something far deeper than any European could imagine. The Gilded One was not a king of a golden city. He was a man offering the most precious thing he had—the light of the sun itself—back to the gods who gave it.
moral: Some treasures cannot be taken.

title: El Pombero: The Goblin of the Argentine Forest
summary: A nocturnal forest spirit punishes those who harm nature and rewards those who show respect.
full_text:
In the northern provinces of Argentina, where the red earth meets the dense forests of Misiones and the Paraná River flows with the force of a thousand horses, the Guaraní people and the gauchos speak of a creature they both fear and respect. He is El Pombero, the Lord of the Night, and his presence is felt by anyone who walks the forest after dark.

El Pombero is tall and thin, with long arms that reach nearly to his knees. His body is covered in coarse hair, and his skin is dark like the bark of the quebracho tree. He wears a wide-brimmed hat made of straw, and his feet point backward, like the Curupira of Brazil. His eyes glow yellow in the darkness, and his voice sounds like the rustling of leaves.

The Pombero is a protector of the forest and its creatures. He punishes hunters who kill animals for sport, leaving their bodies tangled in thorns. He frightens children who torment birds or throw stones at frogs. He steals chickens and eggs from farmers who mistreat their animals, and he causes milk to sour in the udders of cows that are milked without gratitude.

But the Pombero is also a seducer. He is known to visit the homes of beautiful women at night, entering through the smallest cracks. Women who wake with mysterious bruises or who speak in their sleep about a dark figure are said to have been visited by the Pombero. Some women have even borne children with strange features, said to be the offspring of the Pombero.

The only way to protect against the Pombero is to respect the forest. Leave offerings of rum and cigars at the base of a tree. Do not whistle at night, for the Pombero thinks you are calling him. And never, under any circumstances, speak his name aloud after dark — to name him is to invite his presence.

The legend of El Pombero is still alive in rural Argentina. Farmers leave bowls of honey on their porches to keep him happy. Hunters ask his permission before entering the forest. The Pombero is a reminder that the wild places of the earth have their own rules, and those who ignore them do so at their peril.
moral: Respect the natural world, and it will respect you in return.

title: El Lobizón: The Argentine Werewolf
summary: In the vast plains of Argentina, where the pampas stretch to the horizon and the cattle graze beneath a sky that seems too large for the world, there is a curse so deeply believed that the government passed a law to protect its victims.
full_text:
In the vast plains of Argentina, where the pampas stretch to the horizon and the cattle graze beneath a sky that seems too large for the world, there is a curse so deeply believed that the government passed a law to protect its victims. El Lobizón, the Argentine werewolf, is the fate of the seventh consecutive son born to any family.

The curse is specific and unforgiving. When a family has seven sons in a row, with no daughters between them, the seventh son will transform into a lobizón on every full moon night. His transformation is agonizing: his bones crack and reshape, his skin splits and reforms as fur, his teeth lengthen into fangs. He becomes a beast the size of a calf, with eyes that burn like embers and a howl that freezes the blood.

As a lobizón, he roams the countryside. He attacks livestock, ripping open the throats of cattle and horses. He digs up graves and feasts on the dead. He lurks at crossroads, waiting for travelers. His bite is fatal, and those who survive his attack become lobizones themselves.

The belief in the lobizón is so powerful in Argentina that in 1974, the government passed a law making the President of the nation the official godparent of every seventh consecutive son. The President's sponsorship was believed to break the curse. Families registered their seventh sons with the state, and the President would send a gift and a certificate of godparenthood. This tradition continues today, though it has become more symbolic.

There is a story from the province of Corrientes about a farmer named Rodrigo who was the seventh son. On every full moon, his family locked him in a room with iron bars on the windows. One night, the bars broke, and Rodrigo escaped. The next morning, the neighbor's cattle were found dead, their throats torn out. Rodrigo was found sleeping in the barn, his clothes in tatters, his mouth stained with blood. He had no memory of the night.

The only way to kill a lobizón is with a silver bullet blessed by a priest. But most Argentines prefer a simpler approach: avoid traveling on full moon nights, lock your doors at sunset, and pray that the seventh son in your family is a daughter.
moral: Some curses are beyond our control, but compassion can break any spell.

title: El Bacá: The Demonic Familiar of Dominican Folklore
summary: A shapeshifting demon brings wealth to its master in exchange for blood sacrifices.
full_text:
In the rural areas of the Dominican Republic, where the old ways of African spirit worship still mix with Catholicism in a faith called the 21 Divisiones, there is a being that every peasant fears. He is El Bacá, a demonic familiar that can bring wealth but demands a terrible price.

The Bacá is a shapeshifter. It can appear as a black pig with glowing red eyes, a goat with twisted horns, a rooster with feathers like flames, or even a human wearing clothes that never seem to fit. It is created through a pact between a person and dark spirits. The person, called the master, gains wealth and good luck, but the Bacá demands blood.

The Bacá does not demand the master's blood. It demands the blood of a family member. The Bacá must be fed every month, and if the master does not provide a sacrifice, the Bacá will take one of its own accord. A child who dies suddenly, a wife who wastes away, a brother who falls from a tree — these are the payments for the Bacá's favors. When the master dies, the Bacá must be passed to another family member, or it will turn against the household.

The Bacá lives on the property of its master, usually in a special shed or under a tree. It comes out at night to feed. People who hear strange sounds from a neighbor's yard — the grunting of a pig that is not there, the crowing of a rooster at midnight — know that the neighbor has a Bacá.

There is a story from the province of San Juan about a farmer named Juan who found a bag of gold in his field. He used it to buy more land, built a large house, and became the richest man in the region. But every month, one of his animals would die. Then a child in the village. Then his wife. The village priest told Juan that he had made a pact with a Bacá. Juan tried to break the pact, but the Bacá killed him on the spot.

The legend of the Bacá is rooted in the Congo traditions brought to the Dominican Republic by enslaved Africans. It reflects the moral dilemma of wealth gained through questionable means. In a country where poverty is common, the Bacá represents the temptation of easy money and the terrible cost that always comes with it. The story warns: get rich quick, but understand that everything has a price.
moral: Wealth gained through evil means will cost more than you are willing to pay.

title: El Caleuche: The Ghost Ship of Chiloé
summary: A brilliantly lit ghost ship crewed by witches and demons sails the canals of southern Chile, luring sailors to their doom.
full_text:
In the cold, misty channels of the Chiloé Archipelago in southern Chile, where the Pacific Ocean crashes against islands covered in ancient forests, there is a ship that has been sailing for centuries. It appears suddenly, brilliantly illuminated, its decks crowded with laughing figures. It is El Caleuche, the ghost ship of Chilote mythology, and its appearance is an omen of death.

El Caleuche is a three-masted schooner painted white, glowing with a light that seems to come from inside the hull itself. It sails at night, moving against the wind, its sails full even in a dead calm. Music pours from its decks — the sound of violins, harps, and guitars playing melodies that no human has ever composed.

The crew of the Caleuche are not ghosts. They are warlocks and witches who sold their souls for wealth and power. By day, they live normal lives in the villages of Chiloé. By night, they board the Caleuche to feast, dance, and plot their dark schemes. The ship is their meeting place, their floating palace of pleasure.

Any sailor who sees the Caleuche is in danger. If he shows fear, the ship will chase him across the sea, driving him onto the rocks. If he tries to approach, the music will enchant him, drawing him aboard. Once on the Caleuche, he will be forced to join the crew, his soul trapped forever on the ghost ship.

The only way to survive an encounter with the Caleuche is to pretend not to see it. If you look at the ship and immediately look away, the Caleuche will not be able to trap you. The witches and warlocks can only take those who acknowledge their existence.

Some versions of the legend say the Caleuche can transform itself into a rock or a floating log to hide from pursuers. Others say it can sail both on the surface and underwater, disappearing into the depths when threatened. The ship is as elusive as it is terrifying.

The legend of the Caleuche may have been inspired by the ships of the Dutch corsairs who raided the Chilean coast in the 17th century. Or it may reflect the disappearance of the Spanish explorer Alonso de Ercilla's expedition. But for the people of Chiloé, the Caleuche is real. It is the ghost that haunts their islands, the reminder that the sea is full of mysteries and that some ships sail forever without ever reaching port.
moral: The sea holds secrets that are not meant to be seen; some mysteries are best ignored.

title: El Trauco: The Forest Dwarf of Chiloé
summary: A grotesque dwarf-like creature with powers of irresistible seduction haunts the forests of Chiloé.
full_text:
Deep in the forests of Chiloé, where the alerce trees grow so tall that their tops disappear into the mist and the ferns carpet the ground in emerald green, there lives a creature of extraordinary power. He is El Trauco, a dwarf-like being who is one of the most feared figures in Chilote folklore.

El Trauco is small and ugly. He stands no more than three feet tall, with a twisted body, long arms, and legs so short that he seems to crawl rather than walk. His face is hideous — wrinkled, with a huge nose and small, glittering eyes. He wears a hat made of vines and clothes of bark, and he carries a staff called the pahueldún, a magical stick carved from the wood of the alerce tree.

Despite his grotesque appearance, El Trauco has an irresistible power. His gaze can seduce any woman. From a distance, he makes eye contact, and the woman is overcome with desire. She drops whatever she is carrying and walks into the forest, following the Trauco to his hidden cave. There, he seduces her, and she returns to her village hours later with no memory of what happened.

Women who become pregnant after meeting the Trauco are not shamed. In Chilote society, a child fathered by the Trauco is considered blessed — born with special powers, perhaps the ability to see spirits or to heal with herbs. The Trauco provides a socially acceptable explanation for unexpected pregnancies, a supernatural reason that does not require punishment or shame.

But the Trauco is not only a seducer. He is also a punisher. Those who disrespect the forest, who cut trees without permission, or who harm the animals of Chiloé, will feel the Trauco's wrath. He uses his pahueldún to cripple them, striking their legs so that they can never walk properly again.

The Trauco is still believed in by many rural Chilotes. They know to avoid certain parts of the forest, especially at dusk. Women carry scissors in their pockets, believing that the metal repels the Trauco. Men who go into the forest recite a prayer to San Antonio, asking for protection from the dwarf's power.

The Trauco represents the untamed power of the forests of southern Chile. He is the guardian of the wild places, the seducer who protects the trees, and the explanation for the mysteries that still linger in the ancient woods of Chiloé.
moral: The wilderness has its own laws; enter with respect or face the consequences.

title: Egle: The Queen of Serpents
summary: A young woman marries a serpent king and must choose between her husband and her family in Lithuania's greatest folk tale.
full_text:
Egle the Queen of Serpents is the most famous folktale in Lithuania, a story so beloved that its heroine's name is given to girls across the country. It is a story of love, betrayal, and transformation — a story that explains why every tree in the Lithuanian forest has its own character.

Egle was the daughter of a farmer, beautiful and kind. One day, while bathing in a lake, she found a serpent in her clothes. When she tried to remove it, the serpent spoke: "Marry me, Egle, and you will never be sorry." Egle was terrified, but her father insisted she keep her promise to the serpent.

The serpent took Egle to an underwater kingdom beneath the lake. There, he transformed into a handsome young prince named Žilvinas. They married and lived in a golden palace surrounded by gardens of coral. They had three sons and a daughter. Egle was happy.

Years later, Egle's family begged her to visit. Žilvinas agreed but warned: "Do not let them hurt me." Egle returned to her village with her children. Her family was overjoyed, but they also plotted revenge. They did not believe the serpent was really a prince.

Egle's brothers tortured her children until one revealed how to summon Žilvinas. The brothers rowed to the lake and called out: "Žilvinas, come! A deer has stolen your children's clothes!" Žilvinas appeared, and the brothers killed him with scythes.

When Egle learned what had happened, her grief was bottomless. She ran to the lake and called her husband's name. No one answered. The water was red with his blood. Egle cursed her brothers and her family.

The gods took pity on Egle's suffering. They transformed her into a spruce tree, tall and slender, always reaching toward the sky. Her sons became an oak, an ash, and a birch — strong and enduring trees. Her daughter, who had betrayed her father, became a weeping aspen, forever trembling with guilt.

This is why the spruce stands straight and proud, the oak is mighty, the ash is noble, and the aspen trembles at the slightest breeze. When Lithuania was a pagan nation, these trees were sacred. Even today, Lithuanians plant spruce trees in their yards to honor Egle. The story of Egle the Queen of Serpents is the story of every mother, every wife, and every woman who has loved and lost.
moral: Love that crosses boundaries is always at risk; those who cannot accept difference will destroy it.

title: Ebbe Skamelson
summary: Knight Ebbe Skamelson betroths the beautiful Malfred before departing for the Crusades for seven years. Believing him dead, Malfred's mother betroths her to another. Ebbe returns during the wedding feast and in rage kills Malfred, her mother, and the bridegroom. He seeks papal absolution but cannot find peace, and is sentenced to visit all 365 islands in Lake Bolmen in chains. Upon hearing a ballad of his own story, his chains fall off and he dies.
full_text:
Ebbe Skamelson

Upon a small headland which juts from the north into Lake Bolmen, lies an old mansion, Tiraholm, by the peasantry called Tira.

A long time ago there lived here a knight who had a wife and an only child, a beautiful daughter, named Malfred. In the whole country there was not another so fair, and the fame of her beauty traveled far and wide, alluring many suitors to her feet. But Malfred was unmoved by their attentions and turned them away, one after the other.

One day a stately knight, Ebbe Skamelson by name, who had just returned from foreign lands, where he had won his golden spurs, drew up in the court-yard.

With downcast eyes and blushing cheeks the young lady extended her hand when they met, to greet the stranger, who courteously returned her salutation.

The stranger knight became for a time a guest at Tiraholm, and the report soon went out, to the grief of many swains who had indulged in dreams of sooner or later winning the hand of the beautiful maiden, that Ebbe Skamelson and Malfred were betrothed. But, as both were still young, the Knight expressed a desire to join the Crusades to the Holy Land, where he hoped to add to his honors, and stipulated that he be given seven years, at the end of which time he promised to return and celebrate his nuptials.

Some time after Ebbe departed, the old Knight, Malfred's father, died, and it became very lonesome for the daughter and mother in Tiraholm. Year after year passed with no word from Ebbe. The roses of the young maiden's cheeks faded and the dark eyes lost their lustre. The mother advised a remedy and betrothed her to another.

Under the impression that Ebbe had fallen by the sword of the infidels she prepared a wedding feast, and the newly betrothed couple were duly joined according to the rites of the church.

But just as the wedding guests sat themselves at table a gold-laced Knight rode into the court at great speed. The bride became pale under her crown, but the mother, who recognized in the stranger the Knight Ebbe, hastened to meet him in the yard, and reminded him that the seven years had passed, at the same time informing him that his love now sat in the bridal chair with another.

In great anger the Knight sprang to his horse, drew his sword, and after reproaching her for breaking her promise, with one blow he severed her head from her body. His sword still dripping with blood, he sprang from his saddle and into the hall where the festivities were in progress, where the bride sank under his sword, and the bridegroom at another deadly blow fell by her side.

Overtaken by repentance the murderer flung himself upon his horse and rode away into the dark forest, but the pricking of his conscience allowed him no rest. Night and day he saw the apparitions of his victims, and nowhere could he find an escape from them.

Finally he determined to go to Rome, and at the feet of the Holy Father ask absolution from his crimes. A large sum of money procured for him from the Pope the desired indulgence, but absolution from a man did not possess the power to quiet his conscience, still his soul's pain or quell the storm raging in his heart. He then returned to the home of his love, and asked the authorities to impose upon him the severest punishment.

After a long deliberation he was sentenced by the court to be chained hand and foot, in which condition he must visit and pass a day in each one of the three hundred and sixty-five islands in Lake Bolmen. The condemned man went at once about the execution of his sentence. In order that he might get from one island to the other he was given a small boat with which, like a wounded bird, he laboriously propelled himself on his terrible journey.

When, at the end of the year, his sentence was completed he went ashore on the estate of Anglestadt in the district of Sunnebro. Here he went up to a village and rested over night in a barn. Meantime his sorrowful fate had made a deep impression upon the people. A bard had composed a song reciting the woes of Ebbe, and a soothsayer had predicted that upon hearing the song sung Ebbe's chains would fall off and his death follow immediately. While he was lying concealed in the barn, a milkmaid came in the morning to milk the cows. She began to sing "Knight Ebbe's Song," to which he listened with intense interest. At the conclusion of the last verse he cried out with loud voice: "Some is true and some is false."

Thoroughly frightened, the girl sprang into the house and related what had happened. In great haste the people gathered around the barn where Ebbe was lying, commanding him to inform them where he came from and who he was. Still cumbered by his chains he crawled from his shelter and gave his name, at the same time requesting them to conduct him to the churchyard.

Between the village and the church of Anglestadt lies a stone sunken in the ground. When he came to this Ebbe mounted it, raised his eyes to heaven and cried out: "If I am worthy to be buried in consecrated ground, so let it be!"

Instantly the fetters fell from his hands and feet and he sank to the earth a corpse.

Those present took his body and carried it to the church where they buried it in the path outside the churchyard wall, so that all who went into the churchyard should tramp upon his grave. But the next night a long section of the wall, right in front of the grave, was miraculously thrown down. The peasants at once relaid it, but the next night it was again leveled. It was then understood that these happenings were signs that the unfortunate man should be allowed a resting place in consecrated ground, whereupon the churchyard was extended so that the grave was enclosed by its walls, and a low stone even to this day marks the resting place of the outcast. From the fetters, which for a long time hung in Anglestadt church, three iron crosses, resembling the small crosses which were in former times set up in memory of the departed, have been made and placed upon the present church.
moral: True repentance cannot be bought with gold; snip only sincere suffering and divine grace can grant peace to a guilty soul.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: Elvehøj
summary: A young man falls asleep beside an elf-mound and is wooed by elf-maidens who sing beautifully. He resists their enchantments, grasping his sword, and is ultimately rescued by the crowing of a cock at dawn, which awakens him from the spell.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of Elvehøj is told. A young man falls asleep beside an elf-mound and is wooed by elf-maidens who sing beautifully. He resists their enchantments, grasping his sword, and is ultimately rescued by the crowing of a cock at dawn, which awakens him from the spell. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: Life is full of mysteries that demand our respect.

title: Esben and the Witch
summary: Esben is captured by a witch who intends to cook him. Using his wit and bravery, he manages to escape her clutches and outsmart her, eventually returning home safely.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of Esben and the Witch is told. Esben is captured by a witch who intends to cook him. Using his wit and bravery, he manages to escape her clutches and outsmart her, eventually returning home safely. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: Wit and cleverness can overcome raw power.

title: Eyrbyggja Saga - The Haunting of Thorolf Halt-Foot
summary: Thorolf Halt-Foot returns as a malevolent draugr after his death, terrorizing the area around Þórsnes. He is buried, reburied, and even burned, yet he continues to return, even manifesting as a bull. Eventually, his son Arnkel manages to lay him to rest by building a high stone wall around his grave to prevent him from escaping.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of Eyrbyggja Saga - The Haunting of Thorolf Halt-Foot has been told for generations. Thorolf Halt-Foot returns as a malevolent draugr after his death, terrorizing the area around Þórsnes. He is buried, reburied, and even burned, yet he continues to return, even manifesting as a bull. Eventually, his son Arnkel manages to lay him to rest by building a high stone wall around his grave to prevent him from escaping. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: Eyrbyggja Saga - The Guest Thorgunna
summary: The wealthy and sickly Thorgunna is buried with a specific request that her bedding be burned. When a friend, Thorodd, keeps her bed sheets as a gift for his wife, Thorgunna returns as a ghost. She appears in the middle of the night at a farmstead, setting the table and preparing a meal for the living before vanishing back into the grave.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of Eyrbyggja Saga - The Guest Thorgunna has been told for generations. The wealthy and sickly Thorgunna is buried with a specific request that her bedding be burned. When a friend, Thorodd, keeps her bed sheets as a gift for his wife, Thorgunna returns as a ghost. She appears in the middle of the night at a farmstead, setting the table and preparing a meal for the living before vanishing back into the grave. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: Eyrbyggja Saga - The Seal of Frodis-water
summary: The house at Frodis-water is haunted by a group of undead fishermen and their leader, Thorir Wooden-leg. The hauntings escalate until a seal's head begins to emerge from the floorboards. Despite the inhabitants' attempts to smash it back down, the seal keeps rising until a young man uses a sledgehammer to drive it back, temporarily ending the phenomenon.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of Eyrbyggja Saga - The Seal of Frodis-water has been told for generations. The house at Frodis-water is haunted by a group of undead fishermen and their leader, Thorir Wooden-leg. The hauntings escalate until a seal's head begins to emerge from the floorboards. Despite the inhabitants' attempts to smash it back down, the seal keeps rising until a young man uses a sledgehammer to drive it back, temporarily ending the phenomenon. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: Erdlaveersissok the Entrail-Seizer
summary: Erdlaveersissok is a terrifying spirit who haunts the celestial path leading to the moon. She targets travelers who lack composure or discipline. Her most disturbing trait is her habit of stealing the entrails of anyone she can trick into laughing. She serves as a guardian of emotional restraint and a symbol of the perils of the spiritual journey.
full_text: Among the many spirits that guard the paths between worlds, none is more feared than Erdlaveersissok, the Entrail-Seizer. She waits along the narrow bridge from the mortal world to the moon, where shamans travel in their visions. She appears as an old woman, bent and withered, with long claws instead of fingers. Erdlaveersissok has one purpose: to test those who travel the celestial path. She tells jokes, makes faces, contorts her body. If the traveler so much as smiles, the spirit lunges forward and rips out their entrails, stuffing them into a sealskin bag. The only defense is absolute control. The traveler must think of the saddest things. Those who pass her test are granted safe passage to the great mysteries beyond the moon. Those who fail wander the spirit world forever, hollow and incomplete.
moral: The spiritual path demands discipline; a moment's levity can cost everything.

title: Erect Horns and the Sacred Buffalo Hat
summary: Erect Horns (Tomȯsévėséhe) was the prophet of the Só'taeo'o people who later merged with the Cheyenne. He received the Ésevone (Sacred Buffalo Hat) at Tȯhóonévose (Stone Hammer Mountain) near the Great Lakes in Minnesota. The sacred hat represents female power, while the Sacred Arrows represent male power—together they form the two great covenants of the Cheyenne Nation. Erect Horns gave the people the Sun Dance ceremony and convinced the tribe to abandon their sedentary agricultural lifestyle, adopt the nomadic Plains horse culture, and rely on buffalo for survival. His vision transformed the Cheyenne from earth-lodge dwellers to tipi-dwelling Plains warriors.

title: El Chupacabra — The Goat-Sucker
summary: The Chupacabra is a legendary cryptid that first appeared in Puerto Rico in 1995, blamed for mysterious livestock killings in which animals were found drained of blood. Descriptions vary: in Puerto Rico and Latin America it is often depicted as a reptilian, alien-like creature with spines along its back, standing 3-4 feet tall and hopping like a kangaroo, while in the US Southwest it is described as a hairless, dog-like animal with a pronounced spinal ridge, fangs, and claws. The legend spread rapidly through Mexico and the border states of Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona during the late 1990s, with hundreds of alleged sightings and attacks. Scientific investigations have consistently identified the creatures as coyotes, dogs, or raccoons suffering from severe sarcoptic mange, which causes hair loss, thickened skin, and a ghastly appearance. The first eyewitness's description was inspired by the alien creature 'Sil' from the 1995 science-fiction film Species. Despite the debunking, El Chupacabra remains deeply embedded in modern Hispanic folklore throughout the Southwest, representing the mysterious and unexplained in rural life.

title: El Chupacabra in the Texas Borderlands
summary: Throughout the 2000s and 2010s, dozens of alleged Chupacabra carcasses were reported across Texas, particularly in San Antonio, Cuero, Hood County, and Coleman County. Ranchers described finding livestock with puncture wounds and drained of blood, often attributing the attacks to a strange, hairless creature. In Cuero (2007), a hairless carcass named the 'Cuero Chupacabra' gained national attention before DNA tests revealed it was a coyote-wolf hybrid with severe mange. In Hood County (2010), animal control officers shot a creature later identified by Texas A&M as a coyote-dog hybrid. These sightings reflect the blending of ancient Mexican folk belief with modern American cryptozoology, creating a uniquely borderlands legend that continues to evolve with each new discovery.

title: El Coco — The Boogeyman of the Spanish-Speaking World
summary: El Coco, also known as El Coco Man or El Hombre del Costal (the Sack Man), is a bogeyman figure used throughout the Spanish-speaking world to frighten children into good behavior. The figure likely has Moorish origins, derived from the Arabic word for 'fear.' In the Mexican-American and Chicano communities of the Southwest, El Coco is described as a dark, shadowy creature with glowing red eyes who hides in closets, under beds, or in dark corners. Parents warn children: 'Duérmete mi niño, duérmete mi sol, si no viene el Coco y te come el dedo gordo del pie' (Sleep my child, sleep my sun, or the Coco will come and eat your big toe). Some versions describe him as a hunchbacked figure who carries a sack to collect misbehaving children. Unlike many other Hispanic folklore figures, El Coco is purely a disciplinary tool, rarely appearing in narrative stories but persisting as a cultural touchstone across generations of Hispanic families in the United States.
source: Hispanic folklore oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society publications

title: El Cucuy — The Monster Under the Bed
summary: El Cucuy is a distinctly Mexican and Mexican-American variant of the bogeyman legend, particularly prevalent in the US Southwest among Chicano families. Unlike the more generic El Coco, El Cucuy is specifically described as a small, hairy, goblin-like creature who lives under children's beds or in dark closets, waiting to grab ankles or drag away children who refuse to sleep. Some accounts describe him as having red or yellow eyes, long claws, and a mischievous grin. The legend serves dual purposes: as a parenting tool to enforce bedtime and as a cultural rite of passage where children learn to face their fears. In recent decades, El Cucuy has been reimagined in Chicano literature and art as a symbol of the fears faced by immigrant children — the unknown terrors of a new country, border crossing, and cultural displacement.
source: Chicano/Mexican-American oral traditions; Anaya, Rudolfo 'The Curse of the ChupaCabra' (2006)

title: El Sombrerón — The Goblin with the Big Hat
summary: El Sombrerón is a small, mischievous goblin figure from Guatemalan and southern Mexican folklore who also appears in Mexican-American storytelling. He is described as a tiny man — no taller than a child — wearing an enormous hat, a red tie, and riding a tiny horse or mule. El Sombrerón is known for braiding the manes and tails of horses, donkeys, and mules into intricate patterns that cannot be undone. He also bothers women by serenading them under their windows with a tiny guitar, causing them to fall into a deep melancholy or sleeplessness. His enchantment can only be broken by someone who feeds him or by making the sign of the cross. The legend traveled to the US Southwest with Central American and Mexican immigrants, where it persists as a cautionary tale about vanity and the dangers of nighttime wandering. In some versions, El Sombrerón is said to stuff his victims with food and sugar until they die of sweetness.
source: Guatemalan and Mexican folklore; oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: El Duende — The Gnomelike Trickster
summary: El Duende is a small, gnomelike creature from Spanish and Latin American folklore who has a prominent place in Mexican-American oral tradition. Described as a tiny old man or child-like figure wearing a large hat and often carrying a walking stick, El Duende lives in forests, caves, or abandoned buildings. He is known for playing tricks on people — stealing food, hiding objects, and leading travelers in circles through the woods. In some versions, El Duende tries to capture children to keep as his own, especially those who wander away from their parents. In curanderismo, encounters with duendes are believed to cause illness, particularly susto (fright sickness), requiring a limpieza (spiritual cleansing) from a curandero. The duende is also said to have backward feet, making it easy to identify his tracks. Among Hispanic families in the Southwest, stories of El Duende serve to keep children close to home and to explain the mysterious sounds and occurrences of rural life.
source: Spanish and Latin American folklore; Curandero traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: El Nahual — The Shapeshifting Witch
summary: El Nahual (or Nagual) is a shapeshifting witch or sorcerer in Mesoamerican and Hispanic folklore, with a strong presence in Mexican-American tradition. The belief, originating with the Aztecs and other Nahua peoples, holds that certain individuals have the spiritual power to transform into animals — most commonly jaguars, coyotes, dogs, owls, or turkeys. Each person is born with a tonal (animal spirit companion) linked to their birth date in the Mesoamerican calendar, but only those with special power can actually assume the animal's form. Nagualism was practiced by pre-Columbian shamans and integrated with European witchcraft beliefs during the colonial period. In Mexican-American folklore of the Southwest, nahuales are feared for their ability to cause illness, steal livestock, and harm enemies while in animal form. However, in some Indigenous communities, the nahual is seen as a protector of the people and natural resources. The concept was popularized in English by Carlos Castaneda's books, though these accounts are controversial. Today, belief in nahuales persists alongside curanderismo as part of the living folk religion of the borderlands.

title: El Niño Fidencio — The Child Healer of Espinazo
summary: El Niño Fidencio (José de Jesús Fidencio Sintora, 1898–1938) is a Mexican folk saint and curandero whose cult center is in Espinazo, Nuevo León, and whose devotion extends into Mexican-American communities in Texas and California. Orphaned at a young age, Fidencio began performing healings at age 14 and gained fame for his extraordinary cures, which included surgery performed without instruments, trance-channeling of spirits, and the use of mud baths and herbs. He was known for his childlike demeanor — playing with toys, speaking in a high voice, and having his followers call him 'Niño' (child). His healing sessions often included theatrical elements: he would dress as a woman, a doctor, or a bullfighter. After his death in 1938, his devotees established the Fidencista Christian Church. Pilgrimages to Espinazo on his feast day (March 19) draw tens of thousands. In the US, Fidencio altars are maintained in Chicano communities, and his tradition of 'cajones' (trance sessions) continues among espiritista and curanderismo practitioners. His story represents the fusion of folk Catholicism with Indigenous healing traditions that characterizes borderlands spirituality.
source: Mexican folk sainthood traditions; Torres, Eliseo 'Healing with Herbs and Rituals' (2006); Texas Folklore Society

title: El Pacto con el Diablo — Faustian Legends of the Borderlands
summary: Pact-with-the-devil legends are widespread in Hispanic folklore of the US Southwest and northern Mexico, reflecting the deep Catholic influence on borderlands culture. These stories typically involve a desperate or ambitious person who sells their soul to the devil in exchange for wealth, power, love, or musical talent. One famous variant tells of a musician at a border cantina who makes a pact with a mysterious stranger to play the guitar like nobody else — only to find his shadow disappears and his soul is claimed at midnight. In another, a farmer in the Rio Grande Valley trades his soul for a well that never runs dry during drought, a deal that eventually costs him his entire family. The most famous Faustian figure in Hispanic folklore is Juan de Dios, who reportedly signed a contract with the devil in blood to become the greatest charro (horseman) in Jalisco. These legends serve as morality tales about the dangers of pride, greed, and the pursuit of worldly success at the expense of spiritual salvation. They also reflect the harsh economic realities of the borderlands, where poverty often tempted people toward desperate bargains.
source: Mexican and Mexican-American folklore; borderlands oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: El Silbón — The Whistling Specter of the Plains
summary: El Silbón (The Whistler) is a terrifying specter from the folklore of Mexico's northern plains and the US Southwest borderlands. He is described as a tall, thin figure dressed in white, carrying a bundle of bones on his back. His most distinctive feature is his whistle — a melody that begins softly and grows increasingly louder, signaling his approach. Those who hear his whistle must not move; snip if the whistle sounds close, the Silbón is far away, but if it sounds distant, he is right behind you. According to legend, the Silbón was a young man who murdered his father after the father killed the young man's wife in a fit of rage. The grandfather, as punishment, set the dogs on him and cursed him to wander forever carrying his father's bones. In Mexican-American ranching communities, El Silbón is a warning to those who would harm their families, and his story is told to keep children from wandering out at night. His legend has evolved in the borderlands into a uniquely bi-cultural ghost story, blending Mexican spectral traditions with the open landscapes of the American Southwest.
source: Mexican folklore; Texas-Mexico border oral traditions

title: El Tesoro de los Jesuitas — The Jesuit Gold of the Border
summary: The legend of the Jesuit Treasure is one of the most enduring treasure myths of the US Southwest and northern Mexico. When the Spanish Crown expelled the Jesuits from New Spain in 1767, the order was said to have hidden vast stores of gold and silver throughout the Sonoran Desert rather than let it fall into royal hands. The most famous hiding place is said to be in the Sierra Madre, near a cave marked with a cryptic Jesuit symbol — a heart pierced by an arrow above three crosses. Over the centuries, countless treasure hunters — Mexican, Anglo, and Native — have searched for this gold. Some claim the treasure is guarded by the spirit of a Jesuit priest who appears as a ghostly figure in black robes near the hiding place. Others say the treasure tunnels form a network connecting Jesuit missions across the Southwest. In Mexican-American folklore, the legend speaks to the history of Spanish colonization, the power and mystery of the Jesuit order, and the enduring hope of discovering hidden wealth in the desert. The story also reflects the painful history of dispossession, as the treasure rightfully belonged to the Indigenous and Hispanic peoples of the region.
source: Sonoran Desert folklore; Southwestern treasure legends; Texas Folklore Society

title: El Jinete Sin Cabeza del Valle — The Headless Horseman of the Valley
summary: While the headless horseman is a figure known in many cultures, the Hispanic version from the Rio Grande Valley and northern Mexico has unique characteristics. Known as El Jinete Sin Cabeza, this specter is said to be the ghost of a Spanish conquistador or a Mexican rancher who lost his head in battle — either during the Mexican War of Independence or the Mexican-American War. He rides a black horse through the moonlit mesquite at midnight, carrying his head under his arm. Unlike the more playful Halloween version, this headless horseman is a fearsome omen: to see him foretells death, war, or disaster. In some versions, he is searching for his head, which was cut off by Comanche raiders. If you encounter him, you must not look into the eyes of his severed head, or you will be doomed to ride beside him forever. Some Chicano versions of the legend place him at the Battle of the Alamo or at the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, connecting the supernatural figure to the traumatic history of Mexican dispossession in Texas.
source: Texas-Mexican border folklore; oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: El Trauco — The Cursing Dwarf of the Forest
summary: El Trauco is a legendary figure from the folklore of Chiloé, Chile, but his legend has traveled through Latin American diaspora communities into the US Southwest. Described as a small, ugly, deformed dwarf — no more than three feet tall — who wears a hat made of a type of vine, has no teeth, and carries a wooden club or axe. He lives in deep forests and is known for two terrifying powers: his gaze can deform or curse those who meet his eyes, and his breath is believed to possess the power to impregnate young women who wander alone in the woods. According to legend, if a young woman has an unexplained pregnancy, it is blamed on El Trauco. Despite his fearsome reputation, he is also said to be a protector of the forest who punishes those who destroy trees and wildlife. In Chilean-American communities in the US, El Trauco serves as a cautionary figure against premarital sex and as a guardian of old-world traditions in a new environment. His story represents the persistence of rural folklore in urban immigrant communities and the adaptation of Latin American legends to the American landscape.
source: Chilean folklore (Chiloé); Mexican adaptation of Latin American lore; oral tradition

title: Eshu the Trickster and the Two Friends (Yoruba)
summary: Eshu, the divine messenger and trickster, once walked between two farmers who were best friends, wearing a hat that was red on one side and white on the other. The two friends saw him from opposite fields and later argued about the color of the passerby's hat, each insisting on what they had seen. Their argument escalated until they drew weapons against each other. Only then did Eshu reveal himself, explaining that he was teaching them that truth has many sides and that human perception is limited. As the god of crossroads, chance, and communication, Eshu taught the Yoruba that ambiguity is part of life and that patience and humility are essential before judgment. Eshu serves as the messenger between humans and the Orishas, carrying sacrifices to the gods.

title: Enkai and the Gift of Cattle
summary: In the beginning, Enkai, the supreme god of the Maasai, lived on earth with the first people. One day, Enkai decided to give the Maasai a gift more precious than any other — cattle. He lowered them from the sky on a leather thong, and they landed on the green plains below. Enkai instructed the Maasai that they were to be the sole guardians of all cattle in the world, and that these animals would provide them with milk, blood, and meat for eternity. This sacred bond between the Maasai and their cattle remains the cornerstone of Maasai identity to this day.
source: Maasai oral tradition, Encyclopedia of African Religion
moral: Cattle are a sacred trust from the divine, not merely property.

title: Enkai and the Rainbow Serpent
summary: In the time when Enkai still walked among the people, a great drought struck the land. Rivers dried up, grass turned to dust, and the cattle began to die. The Maasai begged Enkai for rain, but their prayers went unanswered. One day, a young girl saw a magnificent rainbow serpent emerge from a dry riverbed. The serpent spoke, saying it would carry a message to Enkai if the people would honor it as a messenger of the divine. The Maasai agreed, and the serpent slithered up the rainbow into the sky. When it reached Enkai, it sang the people's plea. Enkai was moved and sent rain that restored the land. Since then, the rainbow has been seen as a bridge between earth and sky, and the serpent as a sacred messenger between Enkai and the Maasai.
source: Maasai oral tradition, East African folklore archives
moral: All creatures, even those feared, can serve as divine messengers.

title: Erkei-Mergen and the Seven Suns
summary: Long ago, seven suns rose in the sky simultaneously, drying up all rivers and vegetation on earth. Men and animals struggled to survive in the unbearable heat. A famous archer named Erkei-Mergen stepped forward to save the world. Using his divine bow, he shot down the extra suns one by one, restoring balance to the world. The story explains the origin of the single sun and moon in the sky and is one of the most widespread Mongol folktales about the regulation of celestial bodies, reflecting the ancient Mongols' interest in natural phenomena and their origins.

title: Erlig Khan and the Palace of the Dead
summary: Erlig Khan (also Erlik Khan) is the Mongol and Turkic god of death and ruler of the underworld. In one of the most famous Mongol tales, 'The Woman Who Sought Her Husband in the Palace of Erlik Khan,' a brave woman descends into the underworld to rescue her dead husband. She navigates past guardian spirits and offers tribute at the court of the terrifying Erlig Khan, who sits in judgment over the souls of the dead. Through her courage and devotion, she wins her husband's soul back. The story reflects the Mongol belief in a multi-layered cosmos where the underworld is a place of judgment but accessible through shamanic journey and pure intent.

title: Er Sogotokh (The Lonely Man)
summary: A foundational Yakut Olonkho epic about Er Sogotokh, the first human hero created by the supreme deity. Born alone in the Middle World, he must contend with the hostile Abaasy spirits from the Lower World who seek to destroy him. After achieving victory over the evil forces, he finds a wife from the Upper World and establishes the first human tribe. The epic explores themes of solitude, divine purpose, and the establishment of human society in a hostile cosmos. It contains some of the oldest layers of Yakut mythology, predating the northward migration of the Sakha people.

title: Epic of Gesar of Ling
summary: The great Tibetan epic of King Gesar, the longest living epic in the world with over one million verses and 120 volumes. The story recounts the miraculous birth of Gesar, son of deities sent to earth to vanquish demons, depose the powerful, and aid the weak. He has a despised childhood where he appears as an ugly, snotty child called Joru before winning a horse race at age 12 to become king and marry the beautiful 'Brugmo. Mounted on his celestial steed Kyang Go Karkar, he wages campaigns against demon kings, retrieves his kidnapped wife from the King of Hor, and in his eighties descends to Hell before ascending back to heaven. Rooted in pre-Buddhist Bon traditions and dated to the 12th century, it is sung by bard-shamans across Tibet, Mongolia, and Central Asia.

title: Eleggua, the Trickster Messenger
summary: Eleggua (Elegbara) is the trickster orisha of crossroads, doors, opportunities, and fate in Cuban Santería. He is the messenger between humanity and the other orishas, syncretized with the Holy Child of Atocha. Eleggua opens and closes paths, and no ceremony can begin without his permission. He is depicted as a mischievous child who carries a hooked staff and wears a red and black beaded necklace. His number is 3, multiplied by 21. One legend tells how Eleggua created confusion among the orishas by wearing a hat of two colors, causing arguments about which way he was walking — teaching that perspective and truth are often deceptive. Eleggua is considered the first orisha that initiates receive, making him fundamental to all of Santería practice.
source: GoddessGift.com; Aroma de Cuba; Anywhere.com Cuba
moral: Brains can overcome brawn
